data.title: Inocras and Broad Institute Researchers Present New TCGA Whole-Genome Cancer Insights, Accelerating Discovery in Cancer Genomics
data.description:
Inocras and Broad Institute Researchers Present New TCGA Whole-Genome Cancer Insights, Accelerating Discovery in Cancer Genomics

Insights derived from one of the largest datasets with over 250 million harmonized variant calls from over 8,000 tumor-normal whole genomes from more than 30 different cancer types will be presented at the AACR Annual Meeting 2026

Researchers from Inocras, a bioinformatics-led company harnessing the power of whole-genome data and proprietary analytics to advance precision health, and the Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard will jointly present new insights from a collaborative initiative to analyze large-scale whole genome data during the AACR Annual Meeting 2026 in San Diego. The collaboration delivers one of the largest cancer whole-genome analyses from over 8,000 cancer whole-genomes, generated by the NCI’s The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA), across more than 30 cancer types.

This press release features multimedia. View the full release here: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260418386743/en/

New TCGA Whole-Genome cancer insights

For two decades, cancer genomic data has underpinned many of the most important discoveries in cancer genomics. However, most studies have relied primarily on whole-exome sequencing (WES), which covers only ~1–2% of the genome, leaving the majority of the genome unexplored. The Inocras-Broad collaboration analyzes whole-genome sequences (WGS) released by TCGA, delivering an unprecedented, well-curated genomic dataset that can serve as an ideal training set for building artificial intelligence (AI) models to usher in a new era of whole-genome AI-driven precision oncology.

Cancer WGS analysis and dataset comparison

TCGA WGS data was analyzed in parallel with two variant calling pipelines from Broad and Inocras; the December 1, 2025 analysis freeze marks a joint commitment to ensure variant call quality, integrity, and reproducibility. All the data were consolidated into a single frozen dataset to enable a consistent analysis across both groups and robust benchmarking of computational and AI methods.

“With this analysis initiative, we are collectively setting a new standard for cancer genomics,” said the principal investigators (PIs) of the Inocras-Broad collaboration: Drs. Gad Getz, PhD, a professor of pathology at Harvard Medical School, Director of Bioinformatics at the Krantz Family Center for Cancer Research and Dept. of Pathology at Massachusetts General Hospital and Core Institute Member at Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard; Esther Rheinbay, PhD, Assistant Professor of Medicine at Harvard Medical School and Massachusetts General Hospital Cancer Center and Associate Member at the Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard; and Young Seok Ju, MD, PhD, co-founder of Incoras and Associate professor at the Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology. “Whole-exome data has already transformed our understanding of cancer based on a narrow slice of the genome. Now, with harmonized full whole-genome insights, researchers can explore the complete landscape of cancer to identify noncoding driver mutations, genome ploidy assessment, structural variants (SVs) across intergenic and intronic regions, and mutational signatures, among other new insights to further advance translational cancer research.”

Key insights from the Inocras-Broad collaboration

Leveraging high-depth, PCR-free WGS, the new analysis of the somatic mutation landscape in 31 cancer types improved the discovery of new driver events by incorporating the analysis of non-coding regions, genome-wide copy number alterations, and genomic rearrangements.

Some of the insights include: Across all tumors, (i) more than 250 million variants were identified with >1 million somatic SVs, as well as (ii) new coding and non-coding candidate driver mutations, (iii) new genomic signatures of chromosomal instability, (iv) new promoter/enhancer somatic copy number alterations, (v) new patterns of alterations in the previously understudied X and Y chromosomes, and (vi) new candidate SV drivers; moreover, the data show (vii) pathogenic and likely pathogenic germline variants in established cancer predisposition genes affecting about 10% of all cases.

Strong foundation for future cancer research and cancer intelligence

Through the initiative, the PIs have together built a robust foundation to analyze cancer whole genomes at industry-scale. “The Inocras-Broad collaboration reflects our belief that the most impactful discoveries emerge from open, rigorous, and deeply collaborative science,” said Jehee Suh, CEO of Inocras. “Through this partnership, we are not just generating findings—we are building the foundation of a scalable, whole-genome–driven ecosystem for cancer research and clinical translation. I believe this foundation will accelerate discovery, enable clinical adoption, and advance the next generation of cancer intelligence.”

The PIs from Inocras and the Broad Institute will jointly present data highlights and discuss future initiatives during the Exhibitor Spotlight session “TCGA and Beyond: Whole-Genome Data Powering the Next Era of Cancer Intelligence” on Monday, April 20th.

About Inocras

Inocras is a bioinformatics-led company redefining precision health through whole genome data and proprietary analytics. Our oncology and rare disease platforms integrate comprehensive whole genome data with advanced automation to deliver curated and actionable insights at scale that accelerate discovery and diagnostics to improve patient care, bringing a real-world impact. Inocras operates a CLIA/CAP-certified laboratory and partners with leading hospitals, pharmaceutical companies, and research institutions worldwide. For more information, please visit inocras.com and follow the Inocras LinkedIn page.

“The Exhibitor Spotlight Theater is a promotional activity and is not approved for continuing education credit. The content of this Exhibitor Spotlight Theater are the opinions of the presenter and do not represent the position or the opinion of the American Association for Cancer Research® (AACR) or its members.” “Not affiliated with or endorsed by AACR.”

data.title: 2 expensive mistakes most retirees make - and how to avoid them
data.description:
By Robert Powell

'We call them "King Tut" subjects - they're buried with their gold'

When it comes to retirement, many individuals make two costly mistakes: They claim Social Security early, even though it reduces the benefit they could receive by waiting; and they spend less than they can afford.

"They are unwilling to depart with their wealth," said John Duffy, an economics professor at the University of California, Irvine. Duffy and Yue Li, an associate professor of economics at the State University of New York at Albany, co-authored a new study, "Early Social Security Claiming and Slow Asset Decumulation: Experimental Evidence."

And that behavior, Duffy told me, runs counter to the rational-actor model economists typically rely on.

So why does it happen, and can future retirees avoid it?

At a high level, Duffy and Li set out to answer a simple question: Are retirees making rational decisions about Social Security and spending?

They zeroed in on two familiar patterns:

-- Claiming Social Security early, even though waiting typically increases lifetime benefits.

-- Spending savings slowly, even when retirees could afford to spend more.

Both show up consistently in the real world. What's less clear is why. Are people responding to real constraints, such as income needs, health concerns or fears about Social Security? Or are they simply getting it wrong?

Testing retirement decisions in a controlled setting

To isolate the issue, Duffy and Li designed a controlled experiment with people ages 45 to 55.

"They're not retired yet, but they're facing this retirement problem," Duffy said.

The setup mirrored real-life decisions, but stripped away the usual complications. Participants were given a large enough nest egg to cover living expenses. They were told Social Security would remain fully solvent. And health risks were taken off the table.

With those variables removed, the researchers could see whether the behavior would persist on its own.

Participants had to decide when to claim Social Security and how to draw down their savings. Surprisingly, they still claimed early; even when delaying benefits clearly produced more lifetime income, participants opted for the smaller check sooner.

"We incentivized them to try to smooth consumption consistent with a lot of economic models," Duffy said. "We found that even after getting rid of these confounds, people still claimed early."

They also held on to their savings longer than the model predicted. Economists call this the "retirement savings puzzle" - the tendency for households to draw down assets more slowly than theory suggests.

Why retirees underspend

In the real world, underspending is often blamed on uncertainty. People don't know how long they will live, so they err on the side of caution.

But in the experiment, participants were given detailed information about mortality risk. Still, it didn't change their behavior.

"We gave them a lot of information about mortality risk, and they still delayed the reduction in their asset position," Duffy said. "It's this twin phenomenon of early claiming - 'Hurry up and give me my money' - and then very slowly spending it."

Much of this, Duffy said, comes down to what he calls a "wealth preference." Many retirees are reluctant to spend principal. They prefer to live off income - Social Security, dividends, interest - while preserving their nest egg.

That mindset reinforces both behaviors.

"Let's get the money now so I can have more wealth," as Duffy described their thinking. "And then if I want to maintain that wealth, it deters the decumulation of assets in old age."

A gap between theory and behavior

The findings point to a gap between theory and practice.

Lifecycle finance assumes people smooth consumption over their lifetimes and maximize utility or satisfaction.

In practice, they fall short.

"We find that they're getting about 77% of what the rational-actor model would predict," Duffy said. "That's a substantial reduction."

Part of that shortfall stems from claiming Social Security early. Benefits can be reduced by as much as 30% at age 62 relative to full retirement age, and even more compared with age 70.

Duffy also noted that many participants, like retirees in the real world, ended retirement with more wealth than they started with.

"We call them 'King Tut' subjects," he said. "They're buried with their gold."

The phrase is telling. For some, wealth isn't just a resource to be used - it's something to be preserved.

Habit plays a role. After decades of saving, switching gears can be difficult.

"It's something that you're used to doing your whole life," Duffy said. "It may be hard to turn off in old age. And the wealthier you are, the better you are at saving. Turning that around can be tricky."

What retirees can do

The takeaway isn't just about information. It's about behavior.

Many people focus more on avoiding losses than on maximizing lifetime income. Claiming early feels safer; you get the money now and eliminate the risk of leaving benefits on the table.

But that instinct can be costly.

Start with the obvious: Don't default to claiming early. Run the numbers, or have an adviser do it, and understand what delaying actually buys.

"If you have the means to delay early claiming, you should," Duffy said.

Delaying Social Security effectively increases the value of an inflation-protected annuity. Benefits rise from about 70% of full retirement age at 62, to roughly 124% at age 70.

The same logic applies to spending.

Many retirees worry about running out of money. But the risk can cut the other way in not using money they could safely spend. As the authors note, slow drawdowns "can reduce retirement living standards."

A better approach is to build a spending plan, not just a withdrawal rate. Revisit it regularly. Ask whether your current spending supports the life you envisioned, or whether caution is holding you back.

Finally, recognize that a retirement plan isn't just math - it's also behavior. And getting the behavior part right matters just as much as anything else.

-Robert Powell

data.title: New Social Security proposal would cap payments for wealthy people now - and many more later on
data.description:
By Brett Arends

Benefit limits could do a lot to solve Social Security's fiscal problems, CRFB suggests. But check the fine print.

Here's a new proposal to fix Social Security: Cap future payments at $50,000 annually for a single individual and $100,000 for a couple, no matter how much they paid into the system.

This reform idea, put forward by the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget think tank in Washington, could cut up to an estimated $190 billion from benefits for higher earners over the next 10 years, and even more beyond that. The CRFB estimates that if the caps were put in place and frozen for 30 years, that would cut just over half of the program's 75-year budget shortfall.

Those limits - amounting to $4,167 a month for an individual or $8,300 a month for a couple - might seem far removed for retirees and those nearing retirement at the moment.

In the early days, such caps would affect only a small number of well-to-do retirees. But the devil, as always, is in the details. If the limits were frozen in place for 20 or 30 years, which is among the suggestions, inflation would slowly widen the pool.

In today's money, those caps would be worth $3,000 a month for an individual and $6,000 for a couple within a decade, and just $1,900 and $3,800, respectively, in 30 years. (Those calculations are based simply on the average U.S. inflation rates for the past 10 and 30 years.)

For illustration, $1,900 is currently the average individual monthly Social Security payment.

And obviously, the program can only "save" money for Social Security by cutting that money from benefits. The ultimate solution to Social Security's funding crisis is likely to involve some tax increases and some spending cuts.

CRFB's policy proposal comes with an array of options. They assume, by the way, that those limits would apply only to those who start claiming at the so-called full retirement age of 67. Those who start claiming earlier would face lower caps, and those delaying to get the maximum at age 70 would get higher ones.

At the moment, this proposal is just that - a proposal. Is it about to become law? No. But it's important at the moment because it's part of a deepening policy debate about how to fix the finances of Social Security. Without a fix, the program's trust fund will run out of cash in about seven years.

"I don't think anyone is jumping at the gun to make major changes to Social Security in the next year or so," says Marc Goldwein, CRFB's senior policy director. "That said, we are six-and-a-half years from the insolvency of the trust fund. There will almost certainly be Social Security legislation in the next seven years. The question is what's [going to be] in it."

Goldwein adds that in the range of policy proposals, capping benefits for high earners is better than many.

The rest of the federal government can't bail out the program because the rest of the federal government is also in a funding crisis. The official national debt, which does not include the liabilities of the Social Security or Medicare trust funds, is another $31.6 trillion.

This is the result of 25 years of massive federal tax cuts unmatched by any cuts in federal spending. It is sobering to think that in 2000, just before the Bush tax cuts of 2001, the federal budget was in surplus and Washington was seriously discussing what to do with all the extra money it was expecting after protecting Social Security.

Look at the last long-term budget forecast made by the Congressional Budget Office just before the 2000 presidential election. In Table 6 on page 17, you will see that the CBO expected the national "debt" to be negative 40.8% of gross domestic product by 2020 and negative 49.5% by 2030. A negative national debt in this context means a national federal surplus.

But presidential candidate Al Gore, who wanted to prioritize protecting Social Security over tax cuts, lost the Electoral College to George W. Bush, who slashed taxes without cutting spending. As Bush's vice president, Dick Cheney, famously said: "Deficits don't matter." President Trump has followed the same playbook in both of his administrations.

You can see the results here.

Table 1.2 shows that while federal revenues, or taxes, were slashed from 20% of gross domestic product in 2000 to 14.5% by 2009, federal spending rose from 17.7% of GDP to 24.3% over the same period.

Simple calculations from those tables show that if federal taxes had just been left where they were in 2000, at 20% of GDP, that alone might have halved today's national debt. (It would have added $14.4 trillion to revenues.) And that understates the savings, because it also would have cut the $9 trillion in net interest the government has to pay on the national debt over that period.

The rise in federal spending over the same time is more complicated than critics sometimes suggest.

You can see details in Table 8.4. Total discretionary federal spending, 6.1% of GDP in Bill Clinton's last year in office, is 5.9% today (though it rose sharply during the "war on terror," the global financial crisis and the COVID pandemic).

The big rise is in spending on Social Security, Medicare and Medicaid: Together, they've risen from about 10% of GDP to 15%. The main reason for that is simply that baby boomers have retired (and are still retiring). They collect Social Security, Medicare and, in many cases, Medicaid - especially if they end up in a nursing home in their final months or years. Medicaid is the main payer for nursing homes.

President Trump's latest budget unveiled just $73 billion in savings, or 3.7% of the $2 trillion promised to voters just before the 2024 election. (And that's before his proposed $500 billion hike to defense spending.)

Claims that the federal budget is hiding trillions in massive waste, fraud and abuse get repeated at every election, and never come to anything. Claims that vast numbers of fake people were claiming Social Security, repeated frequently last year, also turned out to be completely bogus.

Naturally, these actual facts got a fraction of the coverage of the initial false claims. As Mark Twain supposedly said, a lie can get halfway round the world before the truth can even get its shoes on.

Theoretically, we could save the federal budget from all this spending on seniors through draconian measures. Perhaps the government could replace Social Security with a "DoorDash Grandma" program. Or it could take steps, such as encouraging more obesity and more smoking, to reduce life expectancy.

Otherwise, someone is going to have to find a way to make the budget numbers work - and that will mean pain for somebody. As always, watch the fine print.

-Brett Arends
